input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed as a **reproducible digital twin exemplar**. The physical twin comprises:
- **Core Components**:
  - A **mechanical gantry crane** (scale unspecified but implied to be small/educational/laboratory-sized).
  - An **embedded controller** interfacing with the crane’s actuators and sensors for real-time operation.
- **Operational Domain**:
  - **Industrial automation** and **research/educational use cases**, emphasizing **real-time control, validation, and fault detection**.
  - Operates within a **closed-loop cyber-physical framework**, enabling bidirectional data exchange between the physical and digital domains.
- **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
  - **Modularity**: Supports integration of **kinematic models** (derived from CAD) and **control algorithms** for dynamic simulation.
  - **Sensor-Actuator Interface**: Equipped with sensors (e.g., encoders, position sensors) to capture real-time kinematic and operational data for twin synchronization.
  - **Reproducibility**: Designed to be **open-source** and **cost-effective**, relying on **freely available software** (e.g., simulation engines, visualization tools) to ensure accessibility for academic/research purposes.
  - **Validation Framework**: Previously used as a **demonstrator for continuous validation methods** (e.g., [4], [5]), **fault detection** ([6]), and **system evolution notation (DarTwin)** ([7]), validating its suitability for **real-world applicability** in digital twin research.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides a suite of **real-time and post-processing services** to enhance the physical crane’s performance, monitoring, and decision-making. Key services include:

- **1. Optimal Control Services**:
  - Implements **real-time control algorithms** (e.g., PID, model predictive control) to optimize crane operations (e.g., trajectory planning, load balancing).
  - **Bidirectional feedback loop**: Adjusts physical crane behavior based on digital twin simulations and vice versa.

- **2. Historical Data Logging**:
  - **Time-series database integration**: Logs operational data (e.g., kinematic states, sensor readings, control commands) for **post-analysis**, trend detection, and **predictive maintenance**.
  - Supports **long-term data retention** to enable **historical validation** of system performance.

- **3. Continuous Validation**:
  - **Real-time synchronization**: Compares physical sensor data with **kinematic/dynamic models** in the DT to detect **discrepancies** (e.g., misalignments, wear).
  - Validates **model accuracy** via **residual analysis** and **adaptive calibration** (as demonstrated in [4], [5]).

- **4. Fault Detection and Diagnostics**:
  - **Anomaly detection algorithms**: Identifies **faults** (e.g., actuator failures, sensor drift) by correlating physical data with **simulated twin behavior** ([6]).
  - Provides **root-cause analysis** and **predictive alerts** for proactive maintenance.

- **5. Data Visualization**:
  - **Interactive dashboards**: Renders real-time and historical data (e.g., 3D kinematic trajectories, sensor plots) via **visualization tools** (e.g., MATLAB, custom web interfaces).
  - Supports **user-friendly interfaces** for operators and researchers to monitor system health and performance.

- **6. System Evolution Notation (DarTwin)**:
  - **Structured documentation framework**: Uses **DarTwin notation** ([7]) to model **system evolution**, enabling tracking of **design changes**, **software updates**, and **hardware modifications** in the twin.

- **Target Users**:
  - **Researchers**: For validating **digital twin methodologies** (e.g., synchronization, fault detection).
  - **Industrial Engineers**: For **optimizing crane operations** and **predictive maintenance**.
  - **Educational Institutions**: As a **teaching tool** for cyber-physical systems and digital twin concepts.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** is implemented using a **stack of open-source and industry-standard tools**, ensuring **reproducibility** and **scalability**. Key enablers include:

- **1. CAD and Kinematic Modeling**:
  - **CAD Software**: Likely **FreeCAD, SolidWorks, or Blender** (unspecified but implied for 3D modeling of the crane’s geometry).
  - **Kinematic Simulation**: **Open-source kinematic solvers** (e.g., **Python-based libraries like PyKinematics** or **MATLAB’s Robotics Toolbox**) to model joint trajectories, load dynamics, and constraints.

- **2. Control and Simulation Engines**:
  - **Embedded Control**: **Real-time operating system (RTOS)** or **microcontroller firmware** (e.g., **STM32, Arduino**) for low-latency control of the physical crane.
  - **Digital Twin Simulation**: **MATLAB/Simulink** or **Python (e.g., PyBullet, ROS)** for **dynamic simulation**, **control algorithm prototyping**, and **virtual testing** of scenarios (e.g., load variations, fault injections).
  - **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**: Implemented via **optimization solvers** (e.g., **CasADi, CVXOPT**) for real-time trajectory optimization.

- **3. Data Management and Storage**:
  - **Time-Series Databases**: **InfluxDB, SQLite, or custom CSV/JSON logs** for storing sensor data, control commands, and twin validation metrics.
  - **Cloud/Edge Storage**: Supports **local logging** (for low-latency access) and **cloud synchronization** (for scalability).

- **4. Visualization and User Interfaces**:
  - **3D Visualization**: **MATLAB’s 3D Toolbox**, **Unity/Unreal Engine** (if augmented reality is involved), or **custom Python (Matplotlib/Plotly)** for rendering kinematic states.
  - **Web Dashboards**: **Dash (Python), Grafana, or custom Flask APIs** for real-time monitoring and alerting.

- **5. Communication Protocols**:
  - **Sensor-Controller Communication**: **CAN bus, RS-485, or UART** for interfacing sensors/actuators with the embedded controller.
  - **Twin-Physical Synchronization**: **TCP/IP, MQTT, or ROS (Robot Operating System)** for bidirectional data streaming between the DT and physical system.

- **6. Validation and Testing Tools**:
  - **Fault Injection Framework**: Custom scripts or **ROS-based fault simulators** to test DT robustness under **noise, sensor failures, or actuator drifts**.
  - **Model Validation**: **Statistical tools** (e.g., **Python’s SciPy, MATLAB’s Validation Suite**) for comparing physical data with twin predictions.

- **7. Open-Source and Reproducibility**:
  - **Entirely open-source**: Relies on **freely available software** (e.g., **Python, ROS, MATLAB Student Edition**) to ensure **cost-free adoption**.
  - **Modular Architecture**: Designed for **plug-and-play integration** of new services (e.g., **AI-based predictive maintenance**) without major redesign.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems described in the documents employ an ad-hoc orchestration approach rather than relying on a commercial digital twin framework. The GreenhouseDT exemplar uses virtual machines with pre-recorded data and integrates an InfluxDB time-series database for data storage, paired with an ActiveMQ message broker for asynchronous communication between components. The Air Quality Management exemplar leverages a Raspberry Pi with embedded sensors for real-time data acquisition and an AutomationML-based digital twin, orchestrated via Microsoft Azure tools for simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system, while not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, follows a similar ad-hoc design pattern, utilizing containerization or virtualization technologies for modularity and dissemination. No centralized coordination framework is explicitly mentioned; instead, each exemplar employs lightweight, domain-specific integration mechanisms tailored to their respective use cases (e.g., agricultural, construction, or logistics domains).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems integrate with external systems through specific technologies and protocols. The GreenhouseDT exemplar relies on **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, enabling asynchronous communication between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The Air Quality Management exemplar integrates a **Raspberry Pi** with embedded sensors for real-time data collection and uses **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, with **Microsoft Azure** tools facilitating cloud-based simulation, prediction, and visualization. No explicit mention of interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, ROS 2) or enterprise system integration is provided. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane system is not detailed in the documents, so its horizontal integration specifics remain unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, integrating **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** as a message broker for real-time communication. The system relies on **prerecorded data** within virtual machine images, facilitating easy deployment. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes an **AutomationML-based digital twin** for indoor air quality modeling, with **Microsoft Azure tools** enabling runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar (not explicitly detailed in the provided documents) is implied to use **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, though no specific model/data components are described. In both **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management**, the digital twin pipeline is **ad-hoc**, lacking a standardized commercial framework, and relies on **Raspberry Pi** for sensor integration and **containerization/virtualization** for deployment flexibility. Data sources are primarily **sensor-driven** (e.g., Raspberry Pi sensors for air quality), while model relationships are inferred from **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital components via message brokers (e.g., ActiveMQ).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar employs a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time monitoring of indoor air quality, likely including **environmental sensors** (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, temperature/humidity) to support virus spread prevention. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **prerecorded data** within virtual machines, implying indirect sensing via historical or simulated inputs, but no explicit physical sensors are detailed. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** (not described in the provided documents) is inferred to require **motion sensors** (e.g., encoders) and **positioning systems** for puck/container tracking, though specifics are absent. Sampling frequencies and transmission rates are not mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **behavioral adaptation** of both physical and digital systems, suggesting iterative validation via **real-time feedback loops** enabled by InfluxDB and ActiveMQ. However, explicit **uncertainty quantification**, **error correction mechanisms**, or **quality assurance processes** are not detailed. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **Microsoft Azure** for simulation and prediction, implying **model-driven validation** but lacks specifics on fidelity metrics (e.g., RMSE, accuracy thresholds) or verification methodologies. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** (undocumented) would likely require **kinematic modeling** for fidelity, but no technical details are provided. Overall, the exemplars prioritize **ad-hoc adaptability** over rigorous validation frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin involves real-time data acquisition from the **Physical Crane** hardware, transmitted via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python-based module). Key data types include **sensor measurements** (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the crane and pucks) and **controller telemetry** (e.g., motor currents, joint angles). Communication occurs over unspecified protocols (not detailed in the document), but the **Logging Application** continuously ingests this data into a **TimescaleDB** database. Data preprocessing includes timestamping and structuring for storage, with no explicit mention of filtering or normalization. Data quality assurance is implied through the **Continuous Validation Service**, which later compares logged trajectories with simulated ones. Transmission frequency is not specified, but the system is designed for **continuous logging** (as implied by the 'Logging Application' description). Data integration is facilitated via an **MQTT Broker**, enabling asynchronous communication between services. No explicit event triggers (e.g., threshold-based alerts) are mentioned for this phase, though the **Continuous Validation Service** may indirectly influence data collection by validating logged trajectories against simulations.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** is mediated by the **Control Application**, which generates control commands based on inputs from the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service**. Command types include **optimal movement trajectories** (derived from kinematic models) with no residual swinging motion at endpoints. These commands are transmitted to the **Physical Crane Interface**, which translates them into executable signals for the crane's controllers. Validation and authentication mechanisms are not explicitly detailed, but the **Continuous Validation Service** ensures trajectory fidelity by comparing executed trajectories (logged data) with simulated ones. Feedback systems are implied through the **Logging Application**, which captures real-time physical state data for validation. Conflict resolution is not mentioned, but the system likely relies on the **Continuous Validation Service** to flag deviations (e.g., exceeding acceptable distance metrics) and notify operators via the **Dashboard**. Command execution confirmation is not explicitly described, though the **Logging Application** records the crane's state post-execution, enabling retrospective validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **Digital Twin's Technical Connection** relies on an **MQTT Broker** for inter-service communication, enabling asynchronous, publish-subscribe messaging between containers (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, **Logging Application**). The network topology is not explicitly detailed, but the system is designed for **low-latency interactions** given the real-time nature of crane control and validation. Bandwidth and latency requirements are unspecified, but the **Continuous Validation Service** implies the need for **real-time or near-real-time** data synchronization between physical and digital domains. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are not mentioned, though the use of a **TimescaleDB** (a time-series database) suggests persistence and fault tolerance for logged data. Security protocols are not described, but the **MQTT Broker** likely enforces authentication and encryption for service-to-service communication. The **Physical Crane Interface** acts as a bridge between the digital and physical systems, though its communication protocols with the crane hardware are unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **Digital Twin's Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed, but the system is described as a **software-centric architecture** with containers abstracted via the C4 Model (not Docker containers). The **Database** (TimescaleDB) and **Dashboard** (Grafana) are likely hosted on a **local or on-premise** setup given the context of a lab-scale system. Computational requirements are implied by the **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, and **Continuous Validation Service**, which require real-time kinematic modeling and trajectory comparison. Scalability mechanisms are not mentioned, but the modular container design suggests potential for horizontal scaling if needed. Deployment models are unspecified, though the system is designed to operate alongside the **Physical Crane** (which can function independently). Availability and reliability are ensured through the **Continuous Validation Service**, which monitors trajectory accuracy and alerts operators via the **Dashboard**, though no explicit redundancy or failover mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin systems described in the documents employ **ad-hoc development methodologies** with a focus on **containerization or virtualization technologies** for deployment. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar utilizes **virtual machines (VMs)** preloaded with prerecorded data, enabling rapid setup and replication across environments. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **Raspberry Pi-based sensor systems** for real-time data acquisition and **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, while the **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar (implied by the context) likely follows a similar ad-hoc approach with **C4 Model-based architecture visualization** for system design. Version control is managed via **GitHub repositories** ([GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)), ensuring traceability of code and configuration changes. Evolution management is not explicitly detailed, but the use of **prerecorded data in VMs** and **sensor-driven data pipelines** suggests iterative refinement through real-world validation. Quality assurance practices are inferred to rely on **runtime validation** (e.g., sensor data consistency checks) and **simulation-based testing** (e.g., Air Quality Management’s predictive capabilities). No formalized certification or compliance frameworks are mentioned for lifecycle governance.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **three key decision-support capabilities**:
1. **Runtime visualization** via unspecified tools (likely Azure-based, given Microsoft Azure integration), enabling real-time monitoring of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., particulate matter, CO₂ levels) for virus spread prevention.
2. **Simulation** for predictive modeling of air quality dynamics under varying operational conditions (e.g., ventilation adjustments).
3. **Prediction** of air quality trends using AutomationML-based digital twin models, with confidence measures inferred from simulation accuracy.

The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, enabling **closed-loop control** for environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, humidity). Insights are derived from **InfluxDB-stored time-series data** processed via ActiveMQ message brokering, with decision-making automated via adaptive algorithms (not specified). User interaction is not detailed but likely involves **dashboard-based monitoring** (implied by runtime visualization).

The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** (contextually implied) would likely support **logistics optimization** through real-time tracking of puck (container) movements, with decision-making driven by **sensor-embedded crane control systems** and **C4 Model-aligned architecture** for system monitoring. No explicit analytics or confidence measures are documented.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The provided Digital Twin exemplars exhibit **limited adherence to formal standardization frameworks**:
- **GreenhouseDT**: Relies on **InfluxDB** (time-series database standard) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker standard) but lacks explicit compliance with **ISO/IEC 42010** (system engineering standards) or **IEC 62424** (digital twin standards). No certification requirements are mentioned.
- **Air Quality Management**: Uses **AutomationML** (a standardized exchange format for automation systems, per [14]) for digital twin representation, aligning partially with **IEC 62424** for digital twin modeling. Microsoft Azure tools are employed but not certified under industry-specific frameworks (e.g., **ISO 27001** for security). Interoperability is achieved via **Raspberry Pi sensor integration** and **Azure-based pipelines**, but no explicit adherence to **OPC UA** or **ROS 2** standards is noted.
- **Lab Scale Gantry Crane**: No standardization details are provided in the documents. The **C4 Model** is used for architecture visualization but is not a standardization framework. No industry-specific standards (e.g., **IEC 61512** for cranes) or compliance frameworks are referenced.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail security and safety measures for the **GreenhouseDT** or **Air Quality Management** exemplars. However, the **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (though not explicitly described in the provided documents) is implied to require **cybersecurity protocols** and **safety-critical considerations** due to its **cyber-physical system (CPS) nature**, as inferred from the broader context of digital twin applications in logistics and industrial automation.

For the **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management** exemplars, the following can be inferred from the **technologies used**:
- **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) are employed, which may introduce **data integrity risks** if not secured with **TLS/SSL encryption** or **authentication mechanisms** (not explicitly mentioned).
- The **Raspberry Pi** in the **Air Quality Management** exemplar interfaces with **sensors**, which could be vulnerable to **physical tampering** or **unauthorized access** without documented **access control mechanisms**.
- The **AutomationML-based digital twin** (Air Quality Management) suggests a **structured data model**, but no explicit **data anonymization** or **consent management** policies are described.
- The **Microsoft Azure tools** used in the Air Quality Management exemplar imply reliance on cloud infrastructure, which may require **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **data residency compliance** (e.g., GDPR), though these are not detailed.

**No fail-safe mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, or risk assessment approaches** are explicitly documented for any exemplar. The **ad-hoc nature** of these systems (as opposed to commercial frameworks) likely means **security is not a primary focus**, leaving critical gaps in:
- **Authentication systems** (e.g., OAuth, API keys, or multi-factor authentication).
- **Threat modeling** (e.g., STRIDE analysis for digital twin components).
- **Safety-critical system considerations** (e.g., emergency shutdown protocols for the greenhouse or crane).
- **Compliance with regulations** (e.g., IEC 62443 for industrial IoT security or ISO 27001 for data protection).

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
